46: eli eli lema sabachthani — My God, my God, why have you forsaken me — the Aramaic form (Matthew uses transliterated Aramaic, Mark has Aramaic too; both differ slightly from each other and from the LXX) reproduces the opening of Psalms 22:1. The question hina ti (why, for what purpose) is not a denial of God's sovereignty but a genuine cry from within the experience of abandonment. Jesus prays the whole Psalm, which ends in vindication (Psalms 22:24-31); the cry of dereliction is the beginning of the Passion Psalm, not its whole message. The darkness and the cry together communicate the full weight of bearing divine wrath on behalf of others.
1: prōias de genomenes symbo ulion elabon pantes hoi archiereis kai hoi presbyteroi — when morning came all the chief priests and elders took counsel — Prōias de genomenes (morning having come, genitive absolute) — after the night trial a formal daytime session is required for a legally valid Sanhedrin capital verdict. Symbolion elabon (took counsel, formed a plan) — symbolion (council, deliberation) — the formal morning ratification of the nocturnal vote.
2: kai dēsantes auton apēgagon kai paredōkan Pilatō̄ tō̄ hēgemoni — and binding him they led him away and handed him over to Pilate the governor — Dēsantes (having bound, aorist participle) — binding a prisoner for transport. Apēgagon (led away) and paredōkan (handed over) — the two passion verbs in sequence. Hēgemōn (governor, one who leads) = the Roman prefect’s title; Pilate served as prefect of Judea AD 26-36.
3: tote idōn Ioudas ho paradous auton hoti katekrithē metamelētheis — then Judas who had betrayed him seeing that he was condemned felt remorse — Metamelomai (to feel regret, to have a change of feeling — not the same as metanoeō: repentance leading to restoration). The aorist passive katekrithē (was condemned) — Judas did not anticipate this outcome. Estrēpsen ta triakonta argyria (brought back the thirty silver pieces) — strephō (to turn, return) the coins.
4: hēmarton paradous haima athō̄on — I sinned betraying innocent blood — Hēmarton (I sinned, aorist) — a confession of sin. Haima athō̄on (innocent blood — athō̄os = without penalty, guiltless) is a legal and theological term; shedding innocent blood incurred divine judgment (Deuteronomy 19:10; 1 Kings 2:31). Ti pros hēmas sy opsei (what is that to us? See to it yourself) — the leaders’ dismissal is callous legal language.
5: kai rhipsas ta argyria eis ton naon anechōrēsen — throwing the silver into the sanctuary he departed — Rhipsas (having thrown, cast) — a violent gesture of rejection. Eis ton naon (into the sanctuary/inner temple) — only priests could enter; Judas throws the coins from outside into the sacred space. Apelthōn apēgxato (departed and hanged himself) — apagchomai (to strangle oneself, hang oneself) — cf. Acts 1:18’s different account of the death.
6: hoi de archiereis labontes ta argyria eipan — the chief priests taking the silver said — Ouk exestin balein auta eis ton korbōnan (it is not lawful to throw them into the treasury) — korbōnas (the temple treasury — from Hebrew qorban = offering dedicated to God). The irony: they have no scruple about paying for innocent blood but scruple about returning the money to God’s treasury. Epei timē haimatos estin (since it is the price of blood).
7: symbo ulion de labontes ēgorasan ex autōn ton agron tou kerameos — taking counsel they bought with them the potter’s field — Agros tou kerameos (the potter’s field) — a clay-rich plot used for pottery manufacture, purchased for taphēn tois xenois (burial of strangers/foreigners). The leaders’ decision ironically fulfills prophecy through their own initiative.
8: dio eklēthē ho agros ekeinos Agros Haimatos heōs tēs sēmeron — therefore that field was called Field of Blood until today — Agros Haimatos = Hakeldama (Aramaic, Acts 1:19). The heōs tēs sēmeron (until today) formula marks an etiology — explaining a place-name by its origin story, a common OT narrative device.
9: tote eplērōthē to rhēthen dia Ieremiou tou prophētou — then was fulfilled what was spoken through Jeremiah the prophet — The attribution to Jeremiah is puzzling; the citation conflates Zechariah 11:12-13 with Jer 18-19 (the potter’s field/Hinnom Valley imagery). Some scholars suggest Ieremiou is a scribal error for Zachariou; others note Jeremiah’s purchase of a field (Jer 32) as the deeper background. Matthew’s LXX-influenced citation is a composite prophecy.
10: kai edōkan auta eis ton agron tou kerameos kathōs synetaxen moi kyrios — and gave them for the potter’s field as the Lord directed me — Kathōs synetaxen moi kyrios (as the Lord directed/commanded me) — citing the prophetic word as divine directive. The fulfillment formula closes the Judas-Akeldama parenthesis and returns to the Roman trial narrative.
11: ho de Iēsous estathē emprosthen tou hēgemonos — Jesus stood before the governor — Estathē (aorist passive of histēmi: was placed, stood) — the accused presented before the magistrate. Sy ei ho basileus tōn Ioudaiōn (are you the king of the Jews?) — Pilate’s question shifts from the Jewish blasphemy charge to the Roman sedition charge: king-claiming. Sy legeis (you say so) — the same affirming formula as Matthew 26:25,64.
12: kai en tō̄ katēgoreisthai auton hypo tōn archiereōn kai presbyterōn ouden apekrithē — while being accused by the chief priests and elders he answered nothing — Katēgoreisthai (present passive infinitive of katēgoreō = to accuse — the legal term giving us English “category” via “accusation”). Ouden apekrithē (answered nothing) — the silence of Isaiah 53:7 continues in the Roman proceedings.
13: tote legei autō̄ ho Pilatos ouk akoueis posa sou katamartyrousi — then Pilate says: do you not hear how many things they testify against you? — Posa (how many things, how much) — the number and weight of charges. Pilate’s question implies surprise at the silence; Roman legal procedure expected a defense. Katamartyrousi (testify against, bear witness against — kata = against + martyreō).
14: kai ouk apekrithē autō̄ pros oude hen rhēma — he did not answer him even one word — Pros oude hen rhēma (not even one word) — the absolute totality of the silence. Hōste thaumazein ton hēgemona lian (so that the governor marveled greatly) — lian (greatly, exceedingly) — Pilate’s astonishment. Roman magistrates were experienced with defendants who argued vigorously; Jesus’ silence was extraordinary.
15: kata de heortēn eiōthei ho hēgemōn apolyein hena tō̄ ochlō̄ desmion — at the feast the governor was accustomed to release one prisoner to the crowd — Eiōthei (was accustomed — pluperfect of ethō: was in the habit of, customarily did) — an established Passover custom. No external Roman documentation confirms this practice; it may be a local accommodation or a Passover amnesty arrangement.
16: eichon de tote desmion episēmon legomenon Barabbān — they had at that time a notorious prisoner called Barabbas — Episēmos (notable, notorious — from epi + sēma = marked, distinguished; can be positive or negative) — here clearly negative: a notable criminal. Barabbās = Aramaic bar-ʾabbāʾ (son of the father) — the ironic name-contrast with Jesus Son of the Father.
17: tina thelete apolysō hymin — whom do you wish I release to you? — Pilate’s question frames the choice explicitly. Ton Barabbān ē Iēsoun ton legomenon christon (Barabbas or Jesus who is called Christ) — ton legomenon christon (the one called Christ) — Pilate’s own framing names Jesus by his Messianic title, underscoring the choice between political insurgency and the claimed Messiah.
18: ē̄dei gar hoti dia phthonon paredōkan auton — for he knew that out of envy they had handed him over — Phthonos (envy, malice — the desire to deprive another of good) — Pilate perceives the real motive behind the chief priests’ pressure. Ē̄dei (pluperfect of oida: had known, was aware) — Pilate’s knowledge of the politics.
19: kathēmenou de autou epi tou bēmatos apesteilen pros auton hē gynē autou — while he was sitting on the judgment seat his wife sent to him — Bēma (judgment seat, tribunal — the elevated platform from which a Roman magistrate dispensed justice). The wife’s message is unique to Matthew: mēden soi kai tō̄ dikaiō̄ ekeinō̄ (have nothing to do with that righteous man) — dikaios (righteous, just) — an outsider’s recognition of Jesus’ innocence.
20: hoi de archiereis kai hoi presbyteroi epeisan tous ochlous — the chief priests and elders persuaded the crowds — Epeisan (aorist of peithō = to persuade, convince) — the leaders actively worked the crowd. Hina aitēsōntai ton Barabbān ton de Iēsoun apolēsōsin (to ask for Barabbas and destroy Jesus) — apolēsōsin (aorist subjunctive of apollymi = to destroy, kill).
21: tina thelete apo tōn dyo apolysō hymin — which of the two do you wish I release to you? — Pilate’s repeated offer; hoi de eipan ton Barabbān (they said: Barabbas) — the crowd’s choice is blunt and unqualified. The answer requires no deliberation; the crowd’s demand is immediate.
22: ti oun poiēsō Iēsoun ton legomenon christon — what then shall I do with Jesus who is called Christ? — Pilate’s question frames the crowd as co-decision-makers. Legousin pantes staurōthētō (they all say: let him be crucified) — staurōthētō (aorist passive imperative of stauroō = crucify him) — third-person imperative demanding execution by Roman means (crucifixion was not a Jewish method of execution).
23: ho de ephē ti gar kakon epoiēsen — he said: for what evil has he done? — Pilate’s direct challenge for evidence of wrongdoing. Hoi de perissos ekrāzōn legontes staurōthētō (they cried out all the more saying: crucify him) — perissōs (exceedingly, beyond measure) + ekrazon (imperfect: kept crying out) — escalating, sustained crowd pressure.
24: idōn de ho Pilatos hoti ouden ōphelei alla mallon thorybos ginetai — Pilate seeing that he was accomplishing nothing but rather a riot was developing — Ōphelei (accomplishes, benefits — present indicative: nothing is being achieved by his resistance). Thorybos ginetai (a riot is forming) — the political calculus shifts. Labōn hydōr apenipsato tas cheiras (taking water he washed his hands) — aponiptō (to wash off) — a Jewish gesture of disavowal (Deuteronomy 21:6-9; Psalms 26:6).
25: kai apokritheis pas ho laos eipen to haima autou eph' hēmas kai epi ta tekna hēmōn — all the people answered: his blood on us and on our children — Pas ho laos (all the people) — a corporate acceptance of blood-guilt. To haima autou eph' hēmas (his blood upon us) — the biblical blood-responsibility formula (2 Samuel 1:16; Joshua 2:19). Historically this verse has been abused for anti-Semitic ends; the formula is a self-imprecation by a specific crowd at a specific moment, not an eternal curse on all Jewish people.
26: tote apelysen autois ton Barabbān ton de Iēsoun phragellosās paredōken hina staurōthē̄ — then he released Barabbas, and having flogged Jesus he handed him over to be crucified — Phragellosās (having flogged — from Latin flagellum: the Roman scourge with bone/metal tips) — a brutal preliminary punishment, often fatal on its own. Paredōken hina staurōthē̄ (handed over so that he would be crucified) — final judicial handover.
27: tote hoi stratiōtai tou hēgemonos paralabontes ton Iēsoun eis to praitōrion — then the soldiers of the governor taking Jesus into the praetorium — Praitōrion (the governor’s headquarters — from Latin praetorium): either Herod’s palace in the upper city or the Antonia fortress. Synēgagon ep' auton holēn tēn speiran (assembled the whole cohort against him) — speira (cohort = 1/10 legion, ~600 men; sometimes used for smaller detachments).
28: kai ekdysantes auton chlamyda kokkinēn periethekan autō̄ — stripping him they put around him a scarlet robe — Ekdysantes (stripping, undressing — prior to flogging, garments were removed). Chlamy da kokkinēn (a scarlet robe) — a military/officer’s cloak, mimicking the purple of royalty in mockery. Periethekan (put around him) — the mock-investiture.
29: kai plexantes stephanon ex akanthōn epethēkan epi tēs kephalēs autou — plaiting a crown of thorns they placed it on his head — Plexantes (plaiting, weaving together) — a crown (stephanos = victor’s wreath, not diadēma = royal crown) of thorns twisted together. Kalamōn en tē̄ dexiā̧ autou (a reed in his right hand) — a mock scepter. Gonypetēsantes emprosthen autou enepaixan autō̄ (kneeling before him they mocked him) — the gesture of homage becomes mockery.
30: kai emptysantes eis auton elabon ton kalamōn kai etypton eis tēn kephalēn autou — spitting on him they took the reed and struck his head — Emptysantes (having spat on him) — repeated spitting as in Matthew 26:67. Etypton (imperfect: kept striking) — the reed drives the thorns deeper. The scene enacts the servant’s suffering of Isaiah 50:6 (ton nōton mou edōka eis mastigas kai tas siagonas mou eis rapisma) and Matthew 53:3.
31: kai hote enepaixan autō̄ exedysan auton tēn chlamyda — when they had mocked him they stripped the robe from him — Ekdyō (to strip off, undress) — restoring his own garments before the crucifixion. Kai apēgagon auton eis to staurōsai (and led him away to crucify him) — eis to staurōsai (for the purpose of crucifying) — the infinitive of purpose.
32: exerchomenoi de heuron anthrōpon Kyrēnaion onomati Simōna — going out they found a Cyrenian man named Simon — Kyrēnaios (Cyrenian = from Cyrene in North Africa, modern Libya) — the significant Jewish community in Cyrene made Passover pilgrims from there common. Ton ēngareusan hina arē̄ ton stauron autou (they impressed him to carry his cross) — angareuō (to impress into service, to compel to carry) — from Persian angaros (courier), used for the state’s right to conscript labor.
33: kai elthontes eis topon legomenon Golgotha — coming to a place called Golgotha — Golgotha is the Aramaic transliteration of gulgaltā (skull); the Greek rendering Kranion Topos (Place of a Skull) gives Latin Calvaria. Whether this refers to a skull-shaped hill or a site of execution-related skulls is debated; the location is northwest of the city.
34: edōkan autō̄ pieīn oxos meta cholēs memeigmenon — they gave him wine mixed with gall to drink — Oxyos meta cholēs memeigmenon (wine/vinegar mixed with gall) — cholē (bile, gall — a bitter substance) echoes Psalms 69:21 LXX (kai edōkan eis to brōma mou cholēn). Mark 15:23 has smyrnismenon oinon (wine mixed with myrrh) — a painkiller; Matthew’s cholē emphasizes the bitterness and OT fulfillment.
35: staurōsantes de auton diemerisanto ta himatia autou ballon klēron — crucifying him they divided his garments by casting lots — Staurōsantes (crucifying — aorist participle used absolutely, describing the primary act). Diemerisanto ta himatia (divided the garments) + ballon klēron (casting lots) = Psalms 22:18 LXX verbatim (diemerisanto ta himatia mou heautois kai epi ton himatismon mou ebalon klēron).
36: kai kathēmenoi etēroun auton ekei — and sitting they kept watch over him there — Etēroun (imperfect: kept watching) — the Roman soldiers’ guard duty. The imperfect describes sustained action through the crucifixion hours. This detail confirms Roman military execution and prevents any premature removal from the cross.
37: kai epethēkan epanō tēs kephalēs autou tēn aitian autou gegrammēnēn — and placed above his head the charge against him written — Aitia (charge, accusation, cause — the titulus nailed above the condemned person stating the crime). Houtos estin Iēsous ho basileus tōn Ioudaiōn (this is Jesus the King of the Jews) — the charge is also the ironic proclamation of his true identity.
38: tote staurountai syn autō̄ dyo lē̄stai — then two bandits are crucified with him — Lē̄stai (bandits, robbers, insurgents — cf. Matthew 26:55) placed on either side (heis ek dexiōn kai heis ex euōnymōn = one on the right and one on the left), fulfilling Isaiah 53:12 (en tois anomois elogisthē = he was counted among the lawless).
39: hoi de paraporeuomenoi eblasphēmoun auton kinountes tas kephalas autōn — those passing by were blaspheming him wagging their heads — Kinountes tas kephalas (wagging/shaking their heads) = the gesture of scorn and mockery from Psalms 22:7 LXX (eplēmmēlēsan en cheilesin, ekinēsan kephalēn). Blaspheō (to slander, revile, blaspheme) — the same word used for the high priest’s accusation against Jesus (Matthew 26:65).
40: ho katalyōn ton naon kai en trisin hēmerais oikodomōn — you who would tear down the sanctuary and in three days rebuild it — The temple charge from the false witnesses (Matthew 26:61) is thrown back as a taunt. Sōson seauton ei huios ei tou theou kai katabēthi apo tou staurou (save yourself if you are Son of God, come down from the cross) — the ei huios ei tou theou (if you are Son of God) echoes the devil’s temptation pattern (Matthew 4:3,6).
41: homoiōs kai hoi archiereis empaizōn meta tōn grammateōn kai presbyterōn elepāon — similarly the chief priests mocking with the scribes and elders said — Empaizō (to mock, play with, make sport of) — present participle describing ongoing mockery. All three groups (priests, scribes, elders) now appear together as in the trial scene, providing comprehensive institutional repudiation.
42: allous esōsen heauton ou dynatai sōsai — others he saved; himself he cannot save — The taunt is dramatically ironic: the truth about saving others is conceded, and the inability to save himself is precisely the means by which he saves others. Basileus Israēl estin katabatō nyn apo tou staurou kai pisteusōmen ep' auton (he is King of Israel; let him come down now from the cross and we will believe in him).
43: pepoithen epi ton theon rhysasthō nyn ei thelei auton — he trusted in God; let him rescue him now if he wants him — Pepoithen (perfect of peithō: has trusted and trusts) — citing Psalms 22:8 LXX (ēlpisen epi kyrion rhysasthō auton sōsatō auton hoti thelei auton). The mockers quote the psalm that Jesus will himself pray at Matthew 27:46.
44: to d' auto kai hoi lē̄stai hoi synstaurōthentes syn autō̄ ōneidizon auton — the bandits crucified with him also reproached him in the same way — Ōneidizon (imperfect: were reproaching, taunting) — both bandits initially taunt; Luke 23:39-43 narrates the conversion of one. Matthew’s summary form does not specify the later distinction.
45: apo de hektēs hōras skotos egeneto epi pasan tēn gēn heōs hōras ennatēs — from the sixth hour darkness came over all the land until the ninth hour — Skotos egeneto (darkness occurred, came) — epi pasan tēn gēn (over all the land/earth) — the extent is unclear: all the land of Judea or the entire earth. The three-hour darkness (noon to 3 PM) echoes the Egyptian plague darkness (Ex Matthew 10:22) and Amos 8:9 (kai dysetai ho hēlios mesēmbrias).
47: tines de tōn ekei hestēkotōn akousantes elegon hoti Ēlian phōnei houtos — some of those standing there hearing said: this one is calling Elijah — The mishearing of Ēli as Ēlias (Elijah) reflects either genuine mishearing in the chaos or deliberate misrepresentation. Phōnei (is calling, present tense — continuous cry) — the crowd interprets the cry of dereliction as an appeal to Elijah, the prophet associated with miraculous rescue.
48: kai eutheōs dromōn heis ex autōn kai labōn spongon — immediately one of them running and taking a sponge — Dromōn (running — present participle of trechō, indicating urgency). Plēsas te oxous kai peritheis kalamō̄ epotizen auton (filling it with sour wine and putting it on a reed gave him to drink) — oxos (sour wine, vinegar — the soldiers’ common drink, posca) fulfills Psalms 69:21.
49: hoi de loipoi elegon aphes idōmen ei erchetai Ēlias sōsōn auton — the others said: wait, let us see whether Elijah comes to save him — Aphes (leave, let be, wait) — the spectators hold back to observe whether the Elijah-rescue scenario materializes. The expectation of Elijah-rescue was part of popular eschatology; the watching crowd applies it as a test.
50: ho de Iēsous palin kraxas phōnē̄ megalē̄ aphēken to pneuma — Jesus again crying with a great voice released the spirit — Palin kraxas phōnē̄ megalē̄ (again crying with a great voice) — the physical ability to cry out loudly contradicts the typical physiological description of crucifixion death (asphyxiation). Aphēken to pneuma (released the spirit) — aphiēmi (to let go, release, dismiss) — a volitional act: Jesus yields his spirit rather than having it taken.
51: kai idou to katapetasma tou naou eschisthē ap' anōthen heōs katō eis dyo — and behold the curtain of the sanctuary was torn from top to bottom in two — Katapetasma (curtain, veil) — either the outer curtain (between court and Holy Place) or the inner veil (between Holy Place and Holy of Holies). Ap' anōthen heōs katō (from top to bottom) — the tearing from above signals divine agency. Kai hē gē eseisthē (and the earth shook) — seismōs megas (great earthquake) accompanies the cosmic signs.
52: kai ta mnēmeia aneōchthēsan kai polla sōmata tōn kekoimēmenōn hagiōn ēgerthēsan — and the tombs were opened and many bodies of the saints who had fallen asleep were raised — Kekoimēmenōn hagiōn (saints who had fallen asleep — perfect passive participle of koimaomai = to sleep, the NT euphemism for death). Ēgerthēsan (were raised, aorist passive) — this unique Matthean event has no parallel in other gospels and raises interpretive questions about its historical and theological status.
53: kai exelthontes ek tōn mnēmeiōn meta tēn egersin autou eisēlthon eis tēn hagian polin — and going out of the tombs after his resurrection they entered the holy city — Meta tēn egersin autou (after his resurrection) — the raised saints emerge only after Jesus’ resurrection, not immediately. This protects the prōtotokos ek tōn nekrōn (firstborn from the dead) primacy of Christ. Enephanisthēsan pollois (they appeared to many) — emphanizō (to make visible, manifest).
54: ho de hekatontarchos kai hoi met' autou tēroūntes ton Iēsoun — the centurion and those with him keeping watch over Jesus — Hekatontarchos (centurion, commander of 100) — the officer in charge of the execution. Idōn ton seismon kai ta ginomena ephobēthēsan sphodra (seeing the earthquake and the things happening were greatly afraid). Alēthōs theou huios ēn houtos (truly this was Son of God) — a Gentile soldier’s confession at the cross.
55: ēsan de ekei gynaikes pollai apo makrothen theōrousai — there were many women there watching from a distance — Theōrousai (present active participle: watching, observing — theōreō implies careful attention). Haitines ēkolouthēsan tō̄ Iēsou apo tēs Galilaias diakonousai autō̄ (who had followed Jesus from Galilee serving him) — diakoneō (to serve, minister) — the women’s service parallels the angelic service of Matthew 4:11.
56: en hais ēn Maria hē Magdalēnē kai Maria hē tou Iakōbou kai Iōsēph mētēr — among whom was Mary Magdalene and Mary the mother of James and Joseph — Maria hē Magdalēnē (Mary from Magdala on the western shore of Galilee) — prominent in all four Gospel resurrection accounts. Kai hē mētēr tōn huiōn Zebedaiou (and the mother of the sons of Zebedee) — Salome in Mark 15:40.
57: opsias de genomenes ēlthen anthrōpos ploysios apo Harimathaias — when evening came a wealthy man from Arimathea came — Plousios (wealthy) — Matthew uniquely identifies Joseph as wealthy, possibly evoking Isaiah 53:9 (kai dōsō tous ponērous anti tēs taphēs autou kai tous plousious anti tou thanatou autou = with the rich at his death). Tounoma Iōsēph hos kai autos emathēteuthē tō̄ Iēsou (named Joseph, who also himself was discipled to Jesus) — mathēteuō (to make a disciple of, to disciple) passive: had become a disciple.
58: houtos prosēlthen tō̄ Pilatō̄ ē̄tēsato to sōma tou Iēsou — this one approaching Pilate asked for the body of Jesus — Ē̄tēsato (aorist middle of aitō = asked for himself, requested) — the legal petition for the body of an executed criminal. Roman practice was to leave crucified bodies on the cross; relatives or disciples could request burial. Tote ho Pilatos ekeleusen apodothēnai (then Pilate commanded it to be given up).
59: kai labōn to sōma ho Iōsēph enetylixen auto sindoni kathara — Joseph taking the body wrapped it in a clean linen cloth — Sindōn (fine linen cloth, shroud) — a single piece of linen. Kathara (clean, pure) — ritual cleanliness for burial cloth. The verb enetylixen (wrapped, wound around — from entylissō) describes the wrapping of the corpse for burial.
60: kai ethēken auto en tō̄ kainō̄ autou mnēmeiō̄ ho elalatōmēsen en tē̄ petra — placed it in his own new tomb which he had cut from the rock — Kainō̄ (new, unused — of a kind not previously used) — the newness is significant: no one else had yet been interred there, preventing confusion. Elalatōmēsen en tē̄ petra (had carved out from the rock) — rock-cut tombs in the Judean limestone hills; Joseph’s own prepared tomb. Kai proskyliesas lithon megan tē̄ thyra (rolling a large stone to the door).
61: ēn de ekei Mariam hē Magdalēnē kai hē allē Maria kathēmenai apenanti tou taphou — Mary Magdalene and the other Mary were there sitting opposite the tomb — Kathēmenai apenanti (sitting opposite/in front of) — the two women maintain vigil at the tomb entrance, observing the burial location. Their presence ensures the resurrection witnesses know exactly which tomb is at issue.
62: tē̄ de epaurion hētis estin meta tēn paraskeuēn synēchthēsan hoi archiereis kai hoi Pharisaioi — the next day which is after the Preparation the chief priests and Pharisees were assembled — Paraskeuē (Preparation day = Friday, the day before Sabbath). The Sabbath-day meeting with Pilate underscores the urgency; Pharisees are now mentioned for the first time in the Passion narrative.
63: kyrie emnēsthēmen hoti ekeinos ho planos eipen eti zōn — lord, we remembered that this deceiver said while still living — Planos (deceiver, impostor — from planaō = to lead astray) — the leaders apply the deception vocabulary to Jesus. Meta treis hēmeras egeiromai (after three days I will rise) — their memory of the resurrection prediction is precise; the disciples, by contrast, do not understand it until after the fact.
64: keleuson oun asphalisthēnai ton taphon heōs tēs tritēs hēmeras — command therefore the tomb to be secured until the third day — Asphalizō (to make secure, seal fast — from a + sphallō = without stumbling/faltering) — an official seal to prevent tampering. Mēpote elthontes hoi mathētai autou klepsōsin auton (lest coming his disciples steal him) — the anticipated deception is a stolen body, not a resurrection.
65: ephē autois ho Pilatos echete koustōdian — Pilate said: you have a guard — Koustōdia (guard — from Latin custodia) = a Roman military guard detail. Echete (you have) may be imperative (go ahead, take a guard) or indicative (you have the guard available). Hypagete asphalisasthe hōs oidate (go, make it secure as you know how).
66: hoi de poreuthentes ēsphalisanto ton taphon sphragisantes ton lithon meta tēs koustōdias — they went and secured the tomb sealing the stone with the guard — Sphragizō (to seal — affixing an official clay/wax seal across the stone-door joint, so any tampering would be immediately evident). Meta tēs koustōdias (with the guard) — seal plus military guard provides maximum security, making the resurrection’s impossibility-by-human-means unmistakable.
